title: Articles in Discourse - Part 2
subtitle: Use articles accurately across paragraphs, with abstract nouns, and in high-frequency fixed phrases.

screenSummary.shortDescription: Track a/an and the across paragraphs, use articles with abstract nouns, and handle shared-reference nouns like the internet and the weather.

learningPath.title: Read & Notice
learningPath.instructions: Read each section. Try the embedded Try It tasks before reading on.

learningPath.title: Practise & Build
learningPath.instructions: Work through Activities 1-5. Check your answers in the Answer Key.

learningPath.title: Use It
learningPath.instructions: Complete Activities 6-7. Write and speak in extended, natural language.

objectives: Explain how a/an introduces a referent and the tracks it across a paragraph.
Write articles correctly in a full paragraph, not just isolated sentences.
Choose the correct article with abstract nouns.
Use the correctly before the internet, the weather, the government, and similar high-frequency nouns.
Identify article errors at paragraph level, where context determines the answer.
Write an extended paragraph with accurate article tracking.
Manage article tracking in natural spoken narrative.

theorySections.title: Section 1 - Articles as a tracking system

contentBlocks.text: In real language, articles work across a whole paragraph as a tracking system, not only sentence by sentence.

items: a/an = "I am introducing something new. You do not know which one yet."
the = "You now know which one I mean. We share that information."
Once you introduce something with a/an, every reference to that same thing afterwards uses the.

contentBlocks.title: Tracking system at work
contentBlocks.text: Last week, I rented [a] motorbike from [a] shop near the market. [The] motorbike was almost new, but [the] shop owner warned me that [the] brakes needed checking. I found [a] mechanic nearby. [The] mechanic fixed it in twenty minutes.
items: a motorbike -> introduced for the first time
the motorbike -> we both know which one now
a shop -> introduced for the first time
the shop owner -> the owner of that same shop, now identifiable
a mechanic -> new person introduced
the mechanic -> that same mechanic

rows.situation: First time you mention this noun
rows.example: I spoke to a manager about the contract.
The manager called back the next day.
I bought a motorbike. The brakes needed work.

rows.situation: Category member of something already named
rows.example: We visited a beach resort. The pool was clean.

contentBlocks.title: Using the before anything has been introduced
contentBlocks.incorrect: I moved into the apartment last week. The building was modern.
contentBlocks.correct: I moved into an apartment last week. The building was modern.
contentBlocks.explanation: This is the first mention of the apartment, so the reader does not know which one yet. Use a/an to introduce it, then the afterwards.

contentBlocks.text: Abstract nouns follow the same rules as concrete nouns, but the signals are less obvious. Ask whether the meaning is general or specific and identifiable.

rows.example: Patience is important when dealing with visa applications.

rows.when: A specific instance of the concept that is already known or identifiable
rows.example: The patience she showed during the complaint was impressive.

rows.when: A particular kind or instance of the abstract noun, often with an adjective
rows.example: She has a good knowledge of Thai customs. / It was an experience I will never forget.

rows.general: Advice is useful when you are new here.
rows.specific: The advice she gave me helped a lot.

rows.general: Information about visa renewals is online.
rows.specific: The information in that document was wrong.

rows.general: Progress takes time when learning a language.
rows.specific: The progress she made this month was clear.

rows.general: Experience is valuable in this industry.
rows.specific: The experience I had at that resort changed things.

rows.general: Knowledge of Thai makes daily life easier.
rows.specific: The knowledge he gained saved the project.

contentBlocks.incorrect: She gave me the advice to apply early.
contentBlocks.correct: She gave me advice to apply early. / She gave me the advice I needed to apply early.
contentBlocks.explanation: Use zero article for advice in general. Use the when the specific advice is identifiable.

theorySections.title: Section 3 - Generic the: the internet, the weather, and similar nouns

contentBlocks.text: Some nouns take the because they refer to a concept or system shared by everyone. They are not second mentions; they are already shared references.

rows.why: One global system we all share
rows.example: I do most of my client work over the internet.
The weather in November can be unpredictable.

rows.why: One governing body in a given country
rows.example: The government changed the visa rules last year.
Working remotely is better for the environment.

rows.why: One national or global economic system
rows.example: The economy on the island depends on tourism.
The media covered the new tax rules this week.

items: the internet / the weather / the government: always the because they are shared systems
technology / nature / politics: zero article because they are general abstract concepts
He is interested in technology.
The internet is full of information about it.

practiceTasks.title: Try It A - Second mentions
practiceTasks.instructions: Each gap is the second or later mention of a noun. Write the in every gap.

items.prompt: I signed a new work contract last Friday. ___ contract starts on the first of next month.
She found a language school near Chaweng. ___ school offers evening classes.
We hired a driver for the airport run. ___ driver arrived on time and helped with the bags.
He bought a second-hand laptop. ___ screen had a small scratch, but it worked well.

practiceTasks.title: Try It B - Abstract nouns
practiceTasks.instructions: Write zero article, the, or a/an in the gap.

items.prompt: ___ information about the ferry schedule is on the company website.
___ information he sent me yesterday was out of date.
It takes ___ patience to deal with slow bureaucracy.
I have ___ good knowledge of Word and Excel for this kind of admin work.
___ progress the team made last month was really encouraging.

practiceTasks.title: Try It C - Generic the or zero article
practiceTasks.instructions: Write the or zero article in the gap.

items.prompt: ___ weather during the rainy season makes construction work difficult.
She works in ___ media - she edits online content for a Thai news platform.
___ technology is changing how small businesses manage bookings here.
___ government has introduced a new long-term visa for remote workers.
I use ___ internet to manage all my invoices and client communication.
___ nature on the island is one of the main reasons people stay.

practiceTasks.title: Activity 1 - Trace the tracking system
practiceTasks.instructions: Read the paragraph. Underline every noun phrase with a/an. Then draw an arrow from each underlined noun phrase to the later use of the that refers to the same thing.

items.prompt: How many a/an -> the pairs did you find?

practiceTasks.stimulus: Last month, I took on a new client based in Germany. The client needed a full rebrand for their hotel group on Samui. I found a local graphic designer who agreed to help. The designer had worked with foreign companies before, so she understood what was needed. We set up a project schedule and agreed on a deadline. The deadline turned out to be tight, but we managed. I rented a small meeting room above a cafe in Chaweng for the presentation. The room had a projector, which helped. The client was happy with the result.

practiceTasks.title: Activity 2 - Articles across a paragraph
practiceTasks.instructions: Write a, an, the, or zero article in each gap. Read the whole paragraph first before filling in any gaps.

items.prompt: Gap 1
Gap 2
Gap 3
Gap 4
Gap 5
Gap 6
Gap 7
Gap 8
Gap 9
Gap 10
Gap 11
Gap 12
Gap 13
Gap 14
Gap 15
Gap 16
Gap 17

practiceTasks.stimulus: (1) ___ colleague of mine moved to Samui from (2) ___ UK two years ago. She came with (3) ___ clear plan: she wanted to open (4) ___ small cafe near (5) ___ beach. (6) ___ plan took longer than expected to put into action. First, she needed to find (7) ___ suitable property. After three months, she found (8) ___ place she liked in Ban Tai. (9) ___ landlord was cooperative, and they signed (10) ___ lease quickly. She spent the first month doing (11) ___ renovation work. (12) ___ renovation was more expensive than she had budgeted for, but (13) ___ result was worth it. She now uses (14) ___ internet to manage most of her bookings, and says that (15) ___ experience of starting (16) ___ business on the island has changed how she thinks about (17) ___ risk.

practiceTasks.title: Activity 3 - Abstract nouns: a, the, or zero article
practiceTasks.instructions: Write a, an, the, or zero article in the gap. Each sentence focuses on an abstract noun.

items.prompt: ___ advice the manager gave me was clear and direct.
Moving to a new country always takes ___ courage.
She has ___ impressive knowledge of employment law.
___ progress they have made on the new site is ahead of schedule.
___ patience is essential when you are dealing with visa offices.
That was ___ experience I had not expected to find so useful.
___ information about the new work permit is on the ministry website.
I admired ___ calm she showed when the power went out mid-presentation.

practiceTasks.title: Activity 4 - Generic the or zero article
practiceTasks.instructions: Circle the correct choice. One answer is correct each time.

items.prompt: ___ has made it much easier to manage a business remotely.
Most of my work involves ___ in some way.
___ was perfect for the boat trip.
She studied ___ at university in Chiang Mai.
___ on the island is heavily dependent on tourism.
___ covered the storm damage on the east coast.
I mainly use ___ for video calls and sending invoices.
___ announced changes to the work permit system.

practiceTasks.title: Activity 5 - Paragraph error correction
practiceTasks.instructions: The paragraph contains six article errors. Find each one, write the correction, and note the error type.

items.prompt: Find and correct article error 1.
Find and correct article error 2.
Find and correct article error 3.
Find and correct article error 4.
Find and correct article error 5.
Find and correct article error 6.

practiceTasks.stimulus: Last year, I started working as an online English tutor. A work was slow at first, but I gradually built up a client list. The clients were mostly professionals who needed English for their jobs. After six months, I found a regular student, a finance manager based in Netherlands. Student had a good level but struggled with the business writing. I suggested we work on a actual emails from her job. The advice worked well. The progress she made over three months was the clear sign that the method was right. I also started posting on an internet to promote my tutoring. The response surprised me. I now have a full schedule and have hired a colleague to help.

practiceTasks.title: Activity 6 - Write a paragraph
practiceTasks.instructions: Write a paragraph of 6-8 sentences on one prompt. Then swap with a partner and use the peer review checklist.

items.prompt: Tell the story of how you found your current home, job, or a service provider you use regularly.
Describe a decision you made recently - a purchase, a change of plan, or something you organised.
Describe a challenge you faced when settling into life on Samui, and how you handled it.

supportingPrompts: Does the writer introduce new nouns with a/an?
Does the writer use the when referring back to the same noun?
Are abstract nouns used with the correct article?
Does the paragraph use the internet / the weather / the government correctly, if any of these appear?
Can you find any article errors?

practiceTasks.title: Activity 7 - Spoken narrative
practiceTasks.instructions: Tell your partner a story using one prompt. Try to manage the a/an -> the tracking system naturally as you speak.

items.prompt: Tell your partner about a time you dealt with a difficult situation on Samui or in another country.
Describe how you found a home, a job, or a person who helped you here.
Talk about something you have learned to do since living here - a skill, a habit, or a way of thinking.
What do you think about the economy or the environment on Samui? Give your opinion with examples.
Tell your partner about a plan that did not work out as expected.

supportingPrompts: Round 1: speak for 60 seconds without stopping.
Round 2: your partner asks two follow-up questions.
Listener: write down any article that sounded wrong and discuss it after the round.

entries.explanation: Each gap is a second mention of an already-introduced noun.
Each gap is a second mention of an already-introduced noun.
Each gap is a second mention of an already-introduced noun.
Each gap is a second mention of an already-introduced noun.
A good knowledge = a particular kind.

sampleAnswers: a local graphic designer -> The designer
a small meeting room -> The room
entries.notes: Accept 5-7 pairs depending on how students interpret indirect references such as a full rebrand, a project schedule, and a projector.

entries.explanation: First mention; one cafe among many
The landlord belongs to the place just identified
Second mention of the renovation work
Second mention: the result of that renovation
Risk as a general abstract concept
Specific advice given by the manager
Courage as a general abstract quality
A particular kind of knowledge; adjective signals a/an
Specific calm shown by an identified person

answerKey.teacherNotes: Item 4 distinguishes zero article economics as a discipline from the economy as a system.

answer.correction: The work was slow / Work was slow

entries.errorType: a/an before vowel sound and article choice with plural

answerKey.teacherNotes: Item 2 is an exception to the zero-article proper noun rule. Item 4 may generate discussion; focus on the an/a error as the primary correction.

Teacher-assessed. Use the peer review checklist as the primary assessment tool. Listen for accurate a -> the tracking, correct articles with abstract nouns, and correct use of the internet / the weather / the government.
Teacher-assessed. Listen for tracking errors in real time, especially reusing a/an for a noun already introduced.

selfAssessment: Explain how a/an introduces a noun and the tracks it through a paragraph.
Write articles correctly across a full paragraph.
Choose the correct article with abstract nouns.
Use the correctly before the internet, the weather, the government, and similar nouns.
Tell the difference between zero article technology and the internet.
Find and correct article errors at paragraph level.
Write an extended paragraph with accurate article tracking.
Manage a -> the tracking when speaking naturally.

title: Unless and As Long As
subtitle: Use conditional linkers to talk about restrictions, requirements, and consequences in everyday adult life.

screenSummary.shortDescription: Choose between unless and as long as, rewrite if-not conditions, and avoid common errors in condition clauses.

learningPath.title: Read & Notice
learningPath.instructions: Read each grammar section. Try the embedded Try It tasks before reading on.

learningPath.title: Practise & Build
learningPath.instructions: Work through Activities 1-5. Check your answers in the Answer Key at the end.

learningPath.title: Use It
learningPath.instructions: Complete Activities 6-7. Use your own language and ideas.

objectives: Explain the meaning of unless and as long as.
Choose the correct linker when two options are given.
Rewrite sentences using unless and if...not.
Write the correct verb form in the condition clause.
Identify and correct common errors with both linkers.
Use both linkers naturally in your own speech and writing.

rows.when: First mention of a singular countable noun; job or role
rows.example: I found a co-working space near the market.

rows.when: Second mention; shared knowledge; unique referent; superlative
rows.example: The space opens at 8 a.m. The ferry is the fastest option.

rows.when: General meaning; proper nouns; languages; meals; by + transport
rows.example: Internet access is fast here. She travels by motorbike.

warmUpTask.title: Warm-up - Articles review
warmUpTask.instructions: Write a, an, the, or zero article in each gap.

items.prompt: She is ___ event manager at ___ resort near Chaweng.
I bought ___ motorbike last week. ___ motorbike is almost new.
He travels by ___ ferry to Surat Thani twice a month.
___ weather during the rainy season makes construction work harder.

contentBlocks.text: Unless means if...not. It introduces the one condition that would block or prevent the result. If that condition does not apply, the result happens.

rows.example: Unless you book in advance, you will not get a table on Saturday.
You will not get a table on Saturday unless you book in advance.

rows.unless: Unless you pay the deposit today, they will give the flat to someone else.
rows.ifNot: If you do not pay the deposit today, they will give the flat to someone else.

rows.unless: She will not renew the contract unless the landlord fixes the air conditioning.
rows.ifNot: She will not renew the contract if the landlord does not fix the air conditioning.

contentBlocks.title: Two important rules
items: The condition clause always uses present simple, even when the meaning is future.
Unless already contains the negative idea. Never write unless...not.

contentBlocks.title: Example bank
examples: Unless I leave before 8 a.m., the traffic near Chaweng is terrible.
The doctor will not see you today unless you have an appointment.
My landlord will not fix the leak unless I put it in writing.
We cannot confirm the booking unless the deposit is received by Friday.
Unless the weather improves, the boat trip will be cancelled.
Staff will not get overtime unless they work more than forty hours.

contentBlocks.incorrect: Unless you do not bring your passport, you cannot collect the parcel.
contentBlocks.explanation: Unless already means if...not. Adding do not creates a double negative. The condition clause should be positive.

contentBlocks.text: As long as introduces the positive condition that enables or permits the result. It means on the condition that or provided that.

contentBlocks.formula: As long as = only if / on the condition that.

rows.pattern: As long as + present simple, main clause
rows.example: As long as you keep the noise down, the neighbours will not complain.

rows.pattern: Main clause + as long as + present simple
rows.example: You can park there as long as you move the bike before 8 a.m.

contentBlocks.title: As long as enables; unless blocks
items: As long as you pay on time, the landlord is happy to renew.
Unless you pay on time, the landlord will not renew.
You can borrow the van as long as you fill it up afterwards.
You cannot borrow the van unless you fill it up afterwards.

contentBlocks.title: Key difference
items: As long as = the condition makes something possible or acceptable.
Unless = the condition, if absent, causes a problem or blocks the result.
The condition clause always uses present simple, even when the meaning is future.

contentBlocks.title: Example bank
examples: You can use the scooter as long as you bring it back before dark.
The arrangement works as long as everyone does their part.
As long as the price is fair, I am happy to sign the lease.
We can accommodate the extra guests as long as they book before noon.
As long as the team meets the deadline, the client will not escalate.
The schedule is flexible as long as the total hours are covered.

contentBlocks.incorrect: You can extend the visa as long as you will apply before the expiry date.
contentBlocks.explanation: The condition clause uses present simple, not will + verb, even when the meaning is future.

rows.question: Is the condition something that blocks or prevents the result?
rows.example: Unless you have a visa, you cannot work legally here.

rows.question: Is the condition something that enables or permits the result?
rows.example: You can work here as long as you have the right visa.

rows.question: Either works but the tone is different?
rows.example: As long as focuses on what makes it work. Unless focuses on what would stop it.

contentBlocks.title: Model text - notice both linkers
contentBlocks.text: Hi all, just a reminder about the bike-sharing arrangement. You can use either scooter as long as you sign it out in the log and return it with a full tank. Unless you do this, I will have to put the bikes off limits. The arrangement works well as long as everyone follows the same rules. If someone takes a bike and leaves it near the market without signing back in, I will know. Unless the situation improves by next week, we will hold a team meeting to decide what to do.

practiceTasks.title: Try It A - Rewrite using unless
practiceTasks.instructions: Rewrite each sentence using unless. Keep the same meaning.

items.prompt: If you do not contact the landlord today, the repair will not happen this week.
If the client does not confirm by Thursday, we will have to cancel the reservation.
If I do not leave now, I will miss the last songthaew.

practiceTasks.title: Try It B - Complete with as long as
practiceTasks.instructions: Complete each sentence with as long as and a suitable ending.

items.prompt: You can borrow my laptop as long as ...
The flexible working arrangement is fine as long as ...
As long as ..., I do not mind working the weekend shift.

practiceTasks.title: Try It C - Unless or as long as
practiceTasks.instructions: Write unless or as long as in the gap.

items.prompt: ___ the manager approves the change, we cannot update the rota.
You can reschedule the appointment ___ you give 24 hours notice.
The installation will not be finished on time ___ the parts arrive tomorrow.
___ the team communicates clearly, working remotely is no problem.

practiceTasks.title: Activity 1 - Sort the sentences
practiceTasks.instructions: Read each sentence. Write the sentence number in the correct column.

items.prompt: Unless the contractor calls back today, we will need to find someone else.
As long as the rain holds off, the outdoor event should run fine.
You can stay in the villa as long as you check out before midday.
Unless I hear from the clinic by 5 p.m., I will assume the appointment is confirmed.
The team can work from home as long as client calls are taken in a quiet space.
Unless the landlord agrees in writing, do not sign anything.
I am happy to cover the shift as long as I get the same day off next week.
Unless the ferry leaves before 9 a.m., we will not make the connecting bus to Surat Thani.

practiceTasks.title: Activity 2 - Circle the correct linker
practiceTasks.instructions: Circle the correct word or phrase in brackets. One answer is correct each time.

items.prompt: ___ the weather is good, we usually walk to the market.
The deposit is non-refundable ___ you cancel within 48 hours.
___ you have travel insurance, a trip to the hospital can be very expensive.
You can leave early on Friday ___ your tasks for the week are done.
___ she contacts the recruiter today, the opportunity will probably go to someone else.
I am fine to share the workspace ___ we agree on the schedule first.
___ the power stays on, I can finish the report before midnight.
The outdoor cafe will close early ___ the weather changes.
You can extend the rental by a week ___ the next guest has not already paid a deposit.
___ you follow the check-out instructions, there is no extra charge.

practiceTasks.title: Activity 3 - Write unless or as long as
practiceTasks.instructions: Write unless or as long as in each gap.

items.prompt: ___ you confirm the order before 3 p.m., we can deliver it tomorrow.
The manager will not approve the change ___ it goes through the standard request form.
You can borrow the work phone ___ you return it before the end of the shift.
___ someone volunteers to cover the Saturday market, we will have to close the stall.
The arrangement is fine with me ___ both sides stick to what was agreed.
He will not sign the new contract ___ the probation period is removed.
___ the client pays the invoice by Friday, we will start work on Monday.
I am happy to do the airport run ___ someone else covers my afternoon shift.
___ I can find a reliable house-sitter, I will not be able to take the trip.
The festival stall will be profitable ___ the weather holds and the crowd is big.

practiceTasks.title: Activity 4 - Rewrite with unless or if...not
practiceTasks.instructions: Rewrite each sentence using the linker given. Keep the meaning the same.

items.prompt: If the plumber does not arrive by 10 a.m., we will have to call another one.
Unless the landlord replies this week, I will look at other flats.
If I do not get the contract by Friday, I will follow up on Monday.
Unless the resort upgrades the Wi-Fi, remote workers will not stay long.
If the team does not submit the report by noon, the client will complain.
Unless she renews the work permit on time, she will have to leave the country.

practiceTasks.title: Activity 5 - Error correction
practiceTasks.instructions: Each sentence has one error. Write the corrected sentence and the error type.

items.prompt: Unless the kitchen does not close at 10 p.m., you can still order food.
You can use the company account as long as you will send receipts for everything.
Unless I do not have a visa, I cannot extend my stay legally.
As long as the weather will be good, the boat trip runs every morning.
As long as you do not follow the terms, the contract is valid for one year.
Unless the meeting finishes on time otherwise I will miss the ferry.

practiceTasks.title: Activity 6 - Build from prompts
practiceTasks.instructions: Use the key words to write one natural sentence. Choose the linker that fits the meaning.

items.prompt: you / give 24 hours notice / cancel the appointment / no charge
the rain / stop / the outdoor market / not open today
both sides / agree on the hours / the shared workspace arrangement / work well
she / submit the invoice / this week / the payment / delay until next month
everyone / follow the same process / the handover / go smoothly
I / get someone to cover my shift / I / not make it to the immigration office in time
the team / meet the deadline / the client / extend the contract

practiceTasks.title: Activity 7 - Speaking task
practiceTasks.instructions: Ask your partner the questions. Try to use unless or as long as naturally in your answer each time.

items.prompt: What conditions do you have for borrowing something valuable to you?
Is there anything you would not do at work unless a certain condition was met?
What does a good rental agreement need to include for you to sign it?
What would stop you from recommending a cafe, hotel, or restaurant?
Describe an arrangement that works well in your life right now. What makes it work?
Is there something you want to do soon, but only under certain conditions?

supportingPrompts: After each answer, your partner asks one follow-up question using What if...? And as long as...? What unless...?
Target: 3-4 minutes per pair. Swap roles and do the task again.

entries.answer: Unless you contact the landlord today, the repair will not happen this week.
Unless the client confirms by Thursday, we will have to cancel the reservation.
Unless I leave now, I will miss the last songthaew.

answerKey.teacherNotes: Open answers. Check for as long as + present simple in the condition clause and a permission or positive outcome in the result clause. Samples: as long as you charge it before returning it; as long as the core hours are covered; As long as I get the time off in lieu.

entries.explanation: Absent condition causes loss of deposit
Absent weather change causes early closure
Positive condition enables no extra charge
Sticking to agreement makes it fine
Paying on time enables work to start
Shift coverage makes the run possible

entries.answer: Unless the plumber arrives by 10 a.m., we will have to call another one.
If the landlord does not reply this week, I will look at other flats.
Unless I get the contract by Friday, I will follow up on Monday.
If the resort does not upgrade the Wi-Fi, remote workers will not stay long.
Unless the team submits the report by noon, the client will complain.
If she does not renew the work permit on time, she will have to leave the country.
Unless the kitchen closes at 10 p.m., you can still order food.
entries.errorType: Double negative with unless; remove do not.

entries.answer: You can use the company account as long as you send receipts for everything.
entries.errorType: will in the condition clause; use present simple after as long as.

entries.answer: Unless I have a visa, I cannot extend my stay legally.
entries.errorType: Double negative with unless; remove do not.

entries.answer: As long as the weather is good, the boat trip runs every morning.
entries.errorType: will in the condition clause; use present simple after as long as.

entries.answer: As long as you follow the terms, the contract is valid for one year.
entries.errorType: as long as + do not creates confused meaning; use a positive condition.

entries.answer: Unless the meeting finishes on time, I will miss the ferry.
You can cancel the appointment without a charge as long as you give 24 hours notice.
Unless the rain stops, the outdoor market will not open today.
The shared workspace arrangement works well as long as both sides agree on the hours.
Unless she submits the invoice this week, the payment will be delayed until next month.
As long as everyone follows the same process, the handover should go smoothly.
Unless I get someone to cover my shift, I will not make it to the immigration office in time.
As long as the team meets the deadline, the client will extend the contract.

answerKey.teacherNotes: Other natural versions acceptable. Assess for correct linker choice and present simple in the condition clause.
Teacher-assessed. Listen for correct choice of unless vs as long as, present simple in the condition clause, and absence of double negatives with unless.

selfAssessment: Explain what unless means and when to use it.
Explain what as long as means and when to use it.
Choose the correct linker when two options are given.
Write present simple, not will, in the condition clause after both linkers.
Rewrite sentences using unless and if...not.
Use unless without adding not in the condition clause.
Identify and correct common errors with unless and as long as.
Use both linkers naturally when speaking about conditions and requirements.

title: Reflexive Pronouns
subtitle: Use reflexive pronouns accurately in conversation, writing, and your daily life.

screenSummary.shortDescription: Use the eight reflexive pronoun forms, distinguish object and emphatic uses, and use by + reflexive for alone or without help.

learningPath.title: Read
learningPath.instructions: Work through each Grammar Reference section and complete the Try It tasks before moving on.

learningPath.title: Practise
learningPath.instructions: Complete Activities 1-7. Work carefully; accuracy is the goal.

learningPath.title: Use It
learningPath.instructions: Activities 8 and 9: write about your own life, then speak with a partner.

objectives: Name and use all 8 reflexive pronoun forms.
Use the reflexive function: the subject does the action to themselves.
Use the emphatic function: stress that the subject did it, not someone else.
Use by + reflexive pronoun to mean alone or without help.
Tell the difference between reflexive pronouns and each other.

difficultyGuide.label: Activities 1-2: Recognise the forms and uses

theorySections.title: Grammar Reference 1 - The 8 Reflexive Pronoun Forms

contentBlocks.text: A reflexive pronoun is used when the subject and object of a verb are the same person or thing, or to add emphasis. The exact form depends on the subject of the sentence.

items: Singular forms end in -self: myself, yourself, himself, herself, itself.
Plural forms end in -selves: ourselves, yourselves, themselves.

rows.subject: I
rows.example: I taught myself to dive during the first lockdown.

rows.subject: You (one person)
rows.example: Did you hurt yourself on the motorbike?

rows.subject: He
rows.example: Jake booked the whole villa himself.

rows.subject: She
rows.example: Maria runs the accounts herself.

rows.subject: It
rows.example: The resort app updated itself overnight.

rows.subject: We
rows.example: We organised the beach clean-up ourselves.

rows.subject: You (a group)
rows.example: Help yourselves to the welcome drinks.

rows.subject: They
rows.example: The guests looked after themselves all week.

contentBlocks.title: Common form errors
items: hisself -> himself
theirselves -> themselves
ourself -> ourselves

contentBlocks.text: Reflexive pronouns have two different jobs. The signal test helps decide which use you are looking at.

rows.meaning: The subject does the action to themselves. The subject and object are the same person.
rows.signal: Remove the reflexive pronoun and the sentence breaks or changes meaning.
rows.example: She burned herself on the wok. She burned breaks because it needs an object.

rows.meaning: Stresses who did the action: the subject, not someone else.
rows.signal: You can move it or remove it and the sentence still works.
rows.example: I fixed the air-con myself. I fixed the air-con still makes sense.

contentBlocks.title: Position tip
items: In emphatic use, the reflexive pronoun often comes right after the subject: He himself said it.
It can also come after the object at the end of the sentence: He said it himself.

theorySections.title: Grammar Reference 3 - By + Reflexive and Each Other

contentBlocks.text: by + reflexive pronoun means alone or without help from others. It is different from the emphatic use because the focus is always on the absence of other people or assistance.

rows.meaning: Alone, or without help from others
rows.example: She runs the dive school by herself.

rows.meaning: Stresses who did it; may or may not have been alone
rows.example: She designed the logo herself.

rows.meaning: Two or more people do something to one another; reciprocal in both directions
rows.example: They taught each other basic Thai cooking techniques.

contentBlocks.title: Watch the difference
items: They introduced themselves = each person introduced him/herself individually.
They introduced each other = A introduced B, and B introduced A.

practiceTasks.title: Try It A - Correct reflexive pronoun
practiceTasks.instructions: Complete each sentence with the correct reflexive pronoun.

items.prompt: Marco fell off his scooter and cut ___.
We organised the staff party all by ___.
The resort markets ___ as eco-friendly.
Did you feel proud of ___ after passing the open-water test?

practiceTasks.title: Try It B - Reflexive or emphatic
practiceTasks.instructions: Label each sentence R for reflexive/object use or E for emphatic use.

items.prompt: I burned myself on the wok last night.
The chef himself came out to apologise to the table.
She made herself a coffee before the briefing.
Did you design the whole website yourself?

practiceTasks.title: Try It C - By + reflexive
practiceTasks.instructions: Complete each sentence with by + the correct reflexive pronoun.

items.prompt: He lives on the east coast of the island. He is alone. -> He lives there ___.
I configured the whole POS system. I had no help. -> I set up the system ___.
They rebuilt the beach jetty. No one assisted them. -> They rebuilt it ___.
Can you run tomorrow's island tour without assistance? -> Can you run it ___?

practiceTasks.title: Activity 1 - Production from Situations
practiceTasks.instructions: Read each situation. Write one or two sentences describing what happened or what is true. Use a reflexive pronoun.

items.prompt: Paulo replaced a broken door handle in his rented villa without calling a handyman.
Leila slipped on wet tiles after a long shift and came down hard on her wrist.
Nadia rents a one-bedroom condo near Chaweng, has no flatmates, and prefers it that way.
The regional director flew in and gave the whole presentation personally.
The resort booking platform automatically restored all reservations after a data error.
Tom rewired the distribution board with no electrician and no assistance.

practiceTasks.title: Activity 2 - Both Functions
practiceTasks.instructions: For each prompt, write two sentences: Sentence A uses reflexive object function; Sentence B uses emphatic function.

items.prompt: She + photography / creating something
We + managing a professional challenge
The business or company + its online presence or reputation
He + his living situation on the island
You + a decision you made or a skill you developed

practiceTasks.title: Activity 3 - Fill the Gap
practiceTasks.instructions: Complete each sentence with the correct reflexive pronoun. No word list is provided.

items.prompt: We found ___ stuck in traffic on the ring road at five in the afternoon.
Did you all enjoy ___ at the Songkran party on the beach?
Jake burnt ___ on the barbecue at the staff end-of-season event.
I told ___ it was just a temporary job - that was four years ago.
Maria sees ___ as much more than just a tour guide.
The children helped ___ to fruit at the resort welcome buffet.
You should believe in ___ more - your Thai is really coming along.
The resort positioned ___ as the island's leading eco-lodge.

practiceTasks.title: Activity 4 - Sort It Out
practiceTasks.instructions: Read the sentences. Write the number of each sentence in the correct column.

items.prompt: I found myself a quiet beach on the north side of the island.
The owner himself called to apologise for the booking error.
They live by themselves in a house near Maenam - no flatmates.
Did she organise the whole fundraiser herself?
He locked himself out of his room again - third time this week.
We completed the snorkelling course by ourselves - no instructor.
The rescue cat cleaned itself on the villa steps every morning.
You yourself said it was a good idea at last week's meeting.
Write one original sentence for R using a different verb.
Write one original sentence for E using a different verb.
Write one original sentence for B using a different verb.

practiceTasks.title: Activity 5 - Transform It
practiceTasks.instructions: Rewrite each sentence using the new subject given. Change the reflexive pronoun to match the new subject.

items.prompt: I gave myself a week to think the offer over.
We found ourselves running a small guesthouse on Samui.
He lives by himself on the east coast.
Did you do all that restoration work yourself?
The machine switched itself off after thirty minutes.
I will sort it out myself - do not call anyone.

practiceTasks.title: Activity 6 - Error Correction
practiceTasks.instructions: Each sentence has one error with a reflexive pronoun. Write the correction and name the error type.

items.prompt: We have not seen ourselves since the boat trip last October.
The whole team had to introduce yourself to the new GM at the start of the review.
I found me completely unprepared for the rainy season in my first year here.
Did you manage to sort out the work permit by you in the end?
They texted themselves every day while she was doing the training in Bangkok.
The new chef cooked an entire tasting menu for the owners - she prepared it all by her.

practiceTasks.title: Activity 7 - Expand from Prompts
practiceTasks.instructions: Use the key words as a prompt. Write a complete, natural sentence using a reflexive pronoun.

items.prompt: I / push / start my own business on the island
he / find / managing three Airbnb properties at the same time
they / teach / free-dive / no instructor
she / translate the contract / without any agency
we / cut off from the mainland / during the storm

practiceTasks.title: Activity 8 - Write About Your Life
practiceTasks.instructions: Write one or two sentences for each prompt. Use a reflexive pronoun in each answer.

items.prompt: Something you taught yourself to do since arriving in Samui or your current home.
Something you did completely alone - by yourself - in the past year.
Something you are proud of doing without outside help, using the emphatic form.
A time you found yourself in a situation you never expected.
A skill or habit you and a friend or colleague do for each other.

practiceTasks.title: Activity 9 - Speaking Task
practiceTasks.instructions: Ask and answer these questions with a partner. Use a reflexive pronoun in each answer.

items.prompt: When did you last have to do something completely by yourself?
What is something you have taught yourself since moving to your current home?
Have you ever found yourself living or working somewhere you never expected?
Tell your partner one thing you are proud of doing yourself - no help from anyone.
Do you prefer to live by yourself or with other people? Why?
Describe a moment when you and a friend or colleague genuinely helped each other out.

supportingPrompts: Take turns: both partners ask and answer every question.
When your partner uses a reflexive pronoun correctly, give positive feedback.
When they use the wrong form or forget, correct them gently.
Aim to speak for at least 30 seconds on each question.
Keep a tally of correct reflexive pronouns.

sampleAnswers: He fixed the door handle himself.
He worked it out by himself.
She hurt herself on the wet tiles.
She lives by herself near Chaweng.
She lives on the island by herself.
He gave the whole presentation himself.
The platform fixed itself without any IT input.
He rewired the whole board himself.
Tom did the whole job by himself.

answerKey.teacherNotes: Teacher-assessed. Sentence A must use the reflexive object function; Sentence B must use the emphatic function. Accept plausible, natural sentences.

entries.answer: She gave herself a week to think the offer over.
He found himself running a small guesthouse on Samui.
They live by themselves on the east coast.
Did she do all that restoration work herself?
The machines switched themselves off after thirty minutes.
We will sort it out ourselves - do not call anyone.
We have not seen each other since the boat trip last October.
entries.errorType: ourselves -> each other: seeing is reciprocal

entries.answer: The whole team had to introduce themselves to the new GM at the start of the review.
entries.errorType: yourself -> themselves: whole team has third-person plural reference

entries.answer: I found myself completely unprepared for the rainy season.
Did you manage to sort out the work permit by yourself in the end?
They texted each other every day while she was in Bangkok.
She prepared it all by herself.
I pushed myself to start my own business on the island.
He found himself managing three Airbnb properties at the same time.
They taught themselves to free-dive with no instructor.
She translated the contract herself, without using any agency.
We found ourselves cut off from the mainland during the storm.

answerKey.teacherNotes: Teacher-assessed. Accept plausible sentences that demonstrate each reflexive function clearly.
Teacher-assessed. Monitor pronoun-subject agreement, correct function choice, and natural use of each other vs reflexive.

selfAssessment: Name all 8 reflexive pronoun forms and match them to the correct subject.
Write a sentence using the reflexive object function when given a situation.
Write a sentence using the emphatic function when given a situation.
Write both the reflexive and emphatic functions from the same prompt.
Use by + reflexive pronoun to mean alone or without help.
Tell the difference between reflexive pronouns and each other.
Change the reflexive pronoun correctly when the subject changes.
Identify and correct use-based errors with reflexive pronouns.
Use reflexive pronouns naturally in a conversation with a partner.
